content:
The Verifier Will Reject Your Program

Every eBPF tutorial starts with a program that counts syscalls or prints hello world. None of them explain why your program gets rejected on half your kernels, how to handle the ring buffer filling up under load, or what CO-RE actually does at the binary level. This is what I wish existed before I started.

The eBPF verifier performs static analysis on every program before it loads. It checks for unbounded loops, out-of-bounds memory accesses, uninitialized reads, and invalid helper function calls — by simulating all possible execution paths through your program. If any path is unsafe, the program is rejected. The error messages are often cryptic.

Reading verifier errors

Run bpftool prog load with --debug to see the full verifier log. The log shows register state after each instruction. The verifier tracks whether each register contains a pointer (and what type), a scalar, or an uninitialized value. Type mismatches are the most common rejection cause. The most frequent issues: loops without explicit bounds (use #pragma unroll or a verifier-visible bound), reading a kernel struct field without a null check on the pointer, and using a helper function not available in your program type — kprobe, tracepoint, and XDP have different helper sets.

CO-RE: What It Actually Does

CO-RE (Compile Once, Run Everywhere) solves the kernel version portability problem. Without it, accessing a kernel struct field requires knowing its byte offset in that specific kernel version — an offset that changes between kernel releases as structs are reorganized. CO-RE encodes the field access symbolically in the eBPF program using BTF (BPF Type Format) metadata, and the BPF loader rewrites the actual offset at load time based on the running kernel's BTF.

The practical constraint: CO-RE requires the kernel to be built with CONFIG_DEBUG_INFO_BTF=y. Most modern distributions have this enabled. Embedded kernels, custom builds, and some hardened distributions do not. Before depending on CO-RE in production, verify BTF availability with ls /sys/kernel/btf/vmlinux. If the file does not exist, CO-RE will not work and you need a per-kernel-version compilation strategy or a BTF sidecar.

Kernel range: 5.4 through 6.x

The BPF ring buffer was introduced in Linux 5.8. CO-RE requires BTF which requires 5.2+. The perf buffer (older transport) works back to 4.15 but has worse performance characteristics under concurrent producers. If you need to support kernels older than 5.8, use perf buffer with explicit locking. If you control the kernel version — as in an air-gapped fleet — target 5.8+ and use ring buffer exclusively.

Ring Buffer Back-Pressure

The BPF ring buffer is the correct transport for high-throughput event data from kernel to userspace. It is lock-free, supports variable-length records, and exposes memory-mapped access to userspace so the consumer reads events without a copy. What the tutorials do not cover: what happens when the consumer cannot keep up.

When the ring buffer fills, the kernel drops events and increments a lost event counter. It does not block the kernel-side producer — blocking a syscall path to wait for a slow userspace consumer would defeat the entire purpose of eBPF observability. Your userspace daemon must track lost_events and alert when it is non-zero. An ebpf_events_dropped_total Prometheus counter and an Alertmanager rule that fires when it is sustained above zero for 60 seconds is the minimum viable production setup.

Sizing the ring buffer

Ring buffer size must be a power of two and a multiple of the page size. Start with 4MB for moderate-throughput workloads. The right size is derived from your p99 burst rate: if you see 50,000 events/second at peak and your consumer processes 40,000/second, your buffer needs to absorb the 10,000/second surplus. With an average event size of 64 bytes, a 4MB buffer can absorb roughly 65,000 events — about 6 seconds of surplus at that rate. Tune based on your actual burst duration, not your average rate.

The sched_setaffinity Trick

The single highest-impact optimization for a ring buffer consumer daemon: pin the consumer thread to a dedicated CPU core using sched_setaffinity. Without pinning, the Linux scheduler migrates the consumer thread between cores based on load. Each migration invalidates the L1 and L2 cache entries for the ring buffer memory mapping, causing cache misses on every poll. With pinning, the ring buffer data stays hot in the cache of the dedicated core.

This reduced p99 consumer latency from 340µs to 28µs — a 12× improvement from a 3-line change. It looks too good to be true until you understand that cache misses on a hot ring buffer path dominate the latency budget. The effect is most pronounced on NUMA systems where cross-socket memory access adds 60–100ns per miss.

01

The eBPF verifier is a security boundary, not a performance hint. Programs that pass the verifier are safe to run in kernel context. Programs that fail are rejected entirely — there is no partial execution. Design your programs to be verifier-friendly from the start, not as an afterthought.

02

BTF availability is not universal. Before deploying CO-RE programs to a new environment, check for /sys/kernel/btf/vmlinux. Cloud VMs (AWS Nitro, GCP COS, Azure) all have it. Raspberry Pi OS does not by default. Talos Linux does. Know your fleet.

03

Ring buffer drop rate is the most important operational metric for an eBPF daemon. A drop rate above zero means you are missing events. Missing events in an observability system is worse than having no observability system — you cannot distinguish "nothing happened" from "something happened and we missed it."

04

The sched_setaffinity optimization requires reserving a CPU core exclusively for the consumer daemon. In a fully packed container environment, this may not be possible. In a dedicated monitoring node or a VM with spare capacity, it is the first optimization to apply after ring buffer sizing.
